comments.title: Polar plunge
comments.content: I've signed up with a local club to take a freezing dive into water early next year.  I plan to use this for etheric cleansing.  Do you, or the commentariat, have any recommendations on how I might maximize the benefit of this?  I seem to recall someone mentioning that Eastern Orthodox priests have prayers or blessing they will say over the faithful who take a winter dip in a lake or river.  Any rituals or other preparations I might use?

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content: I know (or, rather, can quickly look up) the Shinto incantation and hand seal (mudra) for this, but I doubt that's suited to your needs. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content: Purely out of curiosity, what is the Shinto incantation and hand seal done for?

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content: In Shinto, what keeps us from proper relations with the kami are conditions of impurity -- tsumi and kegare, in Japanese -- and of the various ways to remove these, cold water is one of the most reliable. The incantations and the hand seal affirm your intention to release all impurities of body and spirit and ask the blessing of certain kami who assist in that release. It's not all that different from folding your hands in prayer and repeating an act of contrition.

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content:
Congratulations on your impending plunge. It's one of my favorite disciplines and having a group improves it dramatically. For getting best results, there are a few things that can be done:

A) Controlled breathing of some type is an excellent preparation. Wim Hof method was created explicitly for this. I've used the fundamental exercise from Systema and JMG's rhythmic / fourfold breathing both to good effect.

B) Regular breathing when you are in the water is extremely helpful. If you are underwater and there is a sensation similar to when you close your throat during a breath hold then your will want to try to get it into circulation again.

C) Accept the fact that you are going to lose heat from your body. Don't fight it and don't tense up. Just accept what's going on. The secret is that relaxation combined with awareness can drastically improve your ability to thermoregulate. The exercise thus becomes both more effective and more comfortable this way; while the willingness to sacrifice means that it is negative energies that tend to come out most easily. Finally, if you are etherically sensitive, don't try to hang on to any of the negative energies being leeched away; instead make a conscious effort to let them go.

D) Rub yourself dry and use friction to warm up. Also, abstain from using any form of hot water. Ideally, electric space heaters would be out as well. Taxing your bodies ability to regulate its own heat appears to be the main trigger for the health benefits. Both the ancient Chinese and Romans considered it a bad (or at least useless) idea to follow a cold wash with a hot one.

As I understand it, the Orthodox use it as part of an annual rite of purification / cleansing. It isn't really treated like a normal discipline but rather a part of a specific rite. The breathing is probably the best preparation, but I imagine prayer could easily be added to it.

Good luck, have fun, become pure!

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content: Thanks so much for this!

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content: Orthodox tend to do this for Epiphany-- Jan 6 (new calendar). For Russians, there's cutting a hole in the ice and plunging into freezing water. For Greeks, there's often a whole thing where prayers are said, a cross is thrown into the water, and the teenage boys dive for it. Other churches have other traditions, but for all Orthodox, it's the day for blessing of water-- generally for all the holy water for the whole year, as well as any nearby lakes, rivers, ponds, bayous etc. You might look up what prayers are said for Epiphany, in case there's anything helpful to you for getting in the right headspace, but... in general, I think only priests can do the blessing of the waters?

replies.content: Thanks for the additional information.  I'll try to find the Orthodox prayers and see if any can be adapted for use by a layman.

Prior and post plunge.

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content:
This is a fantastic practice.

I immerse in Long Island Sound every month for religious purposes, though I am not a Christian so the blessings are probably not fit for your purposes. In the winter and early spring the water is close to freezing, with chunks of ice floating in it.

In my tradition, it is necessary to immerse the full body including the head at least twice, and raise the soles of the feet off the ground so that the water touches every part of the body simultaneously. Depending how cold the water is, you will not want to submerse your head a second time--it will feel Wrong, like you're going to die.

Don't worry, you won't die. Just do it.

Unless you're in the Arctic, the air will actually feel warm when you emerge from the water. That's good. Use that time to dry off thoroughly and then put on clothing, ideally a wool hat. Wool retains heat even when wet. Then go somewhere warm--sometimes hypothermia can set in an hour or two later, so be aware and make sure you pay attention if you start feeling cold or clumsy later on.

As long as you stay warm afterwards, you'll be clear headed and have lots of energy. Use it for good! Best wishes,

Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content: Good heavens, I didn't realize that the mikveh tradition took that robust and outdoor a form. Thanks for the data point!

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content:
Heheheh.

I'll admit it's not the most popular way to observe, but Long Island Sound definitely fits the definition of a kosher mikweh!

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Polar plunge
replies.content: Hi, just want to say that Wim Hof guides people to perform his breathing as a standalone technique.  He explicitly says not to do it before or during full cold exposure.  He does instruct people to do it during cold showers when they've worked up to it. I have passed out from WHM and I  believe it becomes much easier to do so when done in immersive cold exposure.  I just breathe calmly and consciously through the nose when I'm in cold water.

comments.title: Hexagrams and Mercury
comments.content:
A few weeks back you suggested I practice the hexagram rituals as set forth in Circles of Power, invoking (or evoking?) the energy of Mercury to help me cope with a challenging situation involving troublesome landlords. I've had a few shots at tracing the hexagrams, and find them very time-consuming and laborious; having to trace hexagrams for every planet at all four cardinal points leaves my shoulder aching, and also with some sense of frustration at how s-l-o-w-l-y the matter is proceeding. Is there any way of performing the hexagram rituals that's a bit less labor intensive? Perhaps something involving the pseudohexagram? I'm familiar with that since I often invoke the Sun.

I work in the HOGD tradition, with Hellenic deities. The books I have ready for reference are Circles of Power and the Hellenic Golden Dawn; I use the elemental attributions of the pentagrams you devised for the latter book, as those are a helluva lot easier to remember than the traditional GD ones.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content: Um, you don't trace all the hexagrams in each quarter. If you want to invoke Mercury, you trace the hexagram of Mercury in each quarter, full stop, end of sentence.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content: Ah! Thank you. Now all is clear. That makes it a LOT easier. And thank you also for mentioning that invocation, specifically, is what I want.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content: The other secret, which I hope is clear from the book, is that you trace the hexagram clockwise from the Mercury point to invoke. Clockwise invokes, counterclockwise banishes.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content:
Right! I was referencing the Greater Ritual of the Hexagram on pages 149-151 of Circles of Power, 1997 edition, since the Lesser Ritual seems to deal with the elements. I see that according to the diagrams on pages 97-99, the point corresponding to Mercury is the lower left vertex of the upward pointing triangle in the Hexagram, so I’ll go with that as the point of origin for drawing the Hexagram of Mercury, going clockwise for invocation.

I’m wondering how I should structure my ritual. Would it be enough to invoke Mercury at all the cardinal points, vibrating HERMES at each quadrant, then step back to center, perhaps recite a brief prayer, and conclude with the Tau Cross (my answer to the Cabalistic Cross, based on a ritual of that name by John Opsopaus)? Or should I work up something more elaborate? I may be doing this daily for some time, and certainly on Wednesdays.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content: That's correct. I'd recommend starting with something simple, getting fluent with it, and then adding elaborations one at a time.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content: Where can I find the book the Hellenic Golden Dawn? I have not found it online.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content: There isn't one. Some people are making it up as they go along -- which is of course the standard way that magical traditions get founded.

replies.title: Re: Hexagrams and Mercury
replies.content:
JMG encouraged me to write such a book a few years ago, when I began developing this practice. Perhaps I should get started..?

comments.content:
I have a dream of being a writer, and have decided to follow it. I'm well aware that I'll need a lot of practice, hard work, and some decent luck to get there, but for now have decided that a first step is to start writing a blog.

Aside from posting on Wednesdays (the day of Mercury), do you have any simple occult practices you could recommend for setting up and drawing an audience to a blog? (I have just started learning cabalistic magic, and will not be doing anything more complicated than the LRP for several months yet)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Since you're just beginning the work, it's going to be a while before you have the chops to apply magical methods to your blogging. You also don't need to worry about that. As long as you keep three simple things in mind, you'll have no trouble finding your audience.

1) Don't write the same thing that everyone else is writing. You'd be amazed how many people do this and then can't figure out why nobody reads their blogs. Find your own voice and write about the subjects that interest you.

2) Don't try to market yourself. People are tired of hucksters. Just put your posts out there, week after week, and have a tip jar if anyone wants to donate.

3) Keep at it. It'll probably take you a year or more of weekly posts to get an audience -- that's how long it took me. You can use the time to work on your writing skills and develop your own voice.

comments.title: Visualization Training
comments.content:
One of the important skills both for occult practice and non-occult practice is the ability to visualize objects that aren't there. I can do this when I build things in real life, such as my under the stairs storage project I'm doing on Green Wizards right now. I have a harder time with some of the training in the LRM. I came up with these training aids and wondered what you thoughts were on them?

1) Visualize a Color: I've got a collection of paint chips from the hardware store, in the primary colors. I spend time with one each day, looking at it, then closing my eyes and trying to picture that color in my mind. I'm trying to work up from one color to several at the same time.

2) Visualize a Small Ball: I have several small foam balls, cat toys about an 1"-1 1/2" in diameter. I roll the ball slowly around in my hand and fingers. Then I put the ball down and try to visualize the ball in my hand. Rolling through my fingers and sitting in my palm. Sometimes I also put the ball under my foot and roll it with the bare sole, then picture that in my mind.

3) Visualize a Crystal: I have a blue fluorite crystal. It is eight sided like a D&D die. Each side is unique with a different flaw, crack, chip or raised section. I run it through my fingers, feeling each side, and visualize first that face then imagining the next sections to each of the three sides. I try to picture the entire crystal in my mind. Once I get a good picture then I try and draw the sides as they connect on a piece of paper, and compare it to the actual crystal.

4) Visualize a Flame: I watch a candle flame and then close my eyes and try and imagine one in my mind, flickering and moving.

Any others you can think of?

replies.title: Re: Visualization Training
replies.content: These are very good. Another category of visualization training works with lots of things at once -- for example, turn your back on your desk and do your best to imagine everything on it, in its correct position; or imagine yourself walking out into your back yard, if you have one, and see everything that's there. This gives the visual memory a good workout and makes it easier for you to store visual data and make your magical imagery more detailed.

replies.title: Re: Visualization Training
replies.content: Take a look at "Kim's game" in Rudyard Kipling's novel Kim (1901), chapter 9. It's a very effective way of improving both visualization and memory. (The novel, of course, is long out of copyright, and can easily be found on the web.) Wikipedia has an entry on "Kim's Game."

comments.title: Circumcision
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I know this isn't your religion, so it's fine if you don't wish to comment, but could you speculate on what the purpose of circumcision is in Judaism? It seems like far too private a thing to be required for any identification reasons, so I am thinking it must have some other purpose?

The reason I ask is that I am not Jewish, but I had a circumcision for medical reasons when I was 6 - would it affect anything on the subtler planes?

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Circumcision
replies.content: I have no idea. I really don't. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Circumcision
replies.content:
This is a complex question.

It is actually rather timely that you asked, because last Saturday's Torah portion was the section of Exodus in which Moses suffers a spiritual attack in the night and is saved only when his wife, Tzipporah, circumcises their son and anoints Moshe and/or the boy (it's not clear) with the blood.

Tzipporah then declares Moses her "hatan damim", which is a phrase unique to Hebrew that is difficult to translate. Some English translations say "bloody bridegroom". Normally 'hatan' means bridegroom (as in man just married) but it shares a root with "protect", so that the phrase may also indicate he is protected with blood.

In the ancient Near East it was common to smear magical substances on people and buildings to protect them from demonic attack. Judaism has a particular thing about blood. Hence the paschal lamb's blood smeared on the threshold to ward off the Angel of Death during the 10 plagues of Egypt. Priests were also anointed with blood from sacrifical animals (Moses anoints Aaron in this way when he makes his brother High Priest later on.) So one potential reason for circumcision is to anoint with blood to protect the baby from demonic attack (why blood from the genitalia? Unknown.)

In modern normative Judaism the rabbis point to the many places in the Torah that refer to 'circumcising one's heart'. To circumcise your heart means to dedicate yourself to HaShem (that is, the God of Israel), and cut off any love you might have for other gods/idolatry.

There are many more esoteric explanations of these concepts and the practice, but in general the overarching explanation for circumcision is that it is a dedication in blood and flesh of the baby boy to Judaism.

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Circumcision
replies.content:
I was preparing to do start on the Sphere of Protection today and in looking at a comment about Jewish phrasing, I ended up stumbling upon "Sefer Yetzirah: The Book of Creation: In Theory and Practice" by Aryeh Kaplan. As I scrolled down to Chapter 1, line 9, I came across a whole commentary on circumcision that comes out of line 3.

The line translates to:
Ten Sefirot of Nothingness
in the number of ten fingers
five opposite five
with a singular covenant
precisely in the middle
in the circumcision of the tongue
and in the circumcision of the membrum

The commentary explains that "the Hebrew word for 'circumcision' is Milah. This same word, however, also means 'word'". So that "... [w]hen one is given the power of proper speech, his tongue is said to be circumcised."

"circumcision of the membrum" is exactly what you think it is.

In order to understand the significance of this circumcision, one must realize why God commanded that it be done on the eighth day. The Torah states, "On the eighth day, the flesh of his foreskin must be circumcised" (Leviticus 12:3). The covenant of circumcision was originally given to Abraham.

The world was created in six days, representing the six primary directions that exist in a three-dimensional universe. The seventh day, the Sabbath. is the perfection of the physical world, and it represents the focal point of these six directions, as discussed below (4:4). The eighth day then represents a step above the physical, into the realm of the transcendental.

Through the covenant of circumcision, God gave Abraham and his desendents power over the transcendental plane. The most obvious case in which this occurs is in conception, where a soul is brought
down into the world. Since the mark of the covenant is on the sexual organ, it gives the individual access to the highest spiritual realms, from which he can draw down the most lofty souls.

By meditating on the fact that the ten toes represent the Ten Sefirot, one is able to concentrate spiritual energy into the sexual organ. Through such methods, one can gain complete control over one's sexual activities, even in the midst of intercourse. By sanctifying oneself in this manner during sexual intercourse, one is able to determine the qualities of the child that will be conceived.

The covenant of circumcision also represents the channeling of sexual energy. The sexual drive is one of the most poweful psychological forces in man, and when it is channeled along spiritual lines, it can help bring one to the highest mystical states. In giving the commandment of circumcision, God indicated that the emotions and desires associated with sex could be used for the mystical quest of the Divine on a transcendental plane.

The juxtaposition between the "circumcision of the tongue" and the "circumcision of the membrum" explains the prophetic position favored by Elijah. The scripture states, "Elijah went up to the top of the Carmel, entranced himself on the ground, and placed his face between his knees" (l Kings 18:42). This position was used for the intense concentration of spiritual energy. According to the Midrash, this position was used because it placed the head in conjunction with the mark of circumcision.

So there's one answer, fit for Magic Monday!

Normally you would NEVER get this sort of thing for me, but given the synchronicity, who am I to deny a fell student :D. After all, my next post will be to ask our host for help with the SOP (:

comments.title: Scientific proof that magic exists
comments.content:
Good morning, Sir and Druid! This isn't a question, but I just thought I'd log on to show you a scientific, peer-reviewed paper (in case you hadn't seen it) which proves concretely that your (/D. Fortune's) definition of magic is real.
https://www.pnas.org/content/pnas/113/29/8168.full.pdf
The tl;dr -- this paper is easily summarized: bodily consumption of blood sugar in human subjects tracked with _perceived_ time, (sped up or slowed down in accordance with wall clocks that were subtly altered), rather than real time.
So this is an experiment where science and magic agree and reinforce each other. Scientifically speaking, although I'm not a biologist or brain scientist, but as far as I know, it is perfectly reasonable to think of the brain as analogous to a combustion engine, and blood sugar (glucose) is the only fuel which it burns. If your brain engine is "revving faster", due to an accelerated perception of time, then it consumes more fuel. Less fuel if it is "revving slower". Perfectly logical, and proven in a lab by this experiment.
But speaking as a diabetic myself, (mine is well-controlled, but I had a bad incident once, as with many diabetics, which forced me to pay attention to blood sugar) -- blood sugar has an _IMMENSE_ impact upon one's mood, and therefore perceptions. An immense impact, real, concrete, but difficult to quantify with formulae. Optimal blood sugar produces clarity and efficiency of thought -- too high or too low, in my experience, and a person will experience either "brain fog" (shutting down of perceptions), or anger and irritability (altered perceptions), etc. It seems to vary a lot with the person. (And a longstanding theory of mine is that the excessive amounts of sugar in the American diet contribute to the peculiar American irrationality that even the most obtuse observers are forced to acknowledge these days.)
It should go without saying that, although this experiment used wall clocks that were subtly altered to run fast or slow... a competent magician can produce the same effect at will with non-mechanical means. Launch into a boring speech, and your subjects will perceive time as slower, etc.
So there you have it. Next time you encounter a "scientific skeptic" who doesn't believe D. Fortune's definition of magic is valid, you can show them this paper. If they are willing to admit that blood sugar holds a large influence over brain function, then, case closed.
I searched your dreamwidth archives for "blood sugar" and found no entries, so I am hoping you haven't seen this paper before. If you're aware of it, pardon the repetition.

replies.title: Re: Scientific proof that magic exists
replies.content: Thanks for this! It's fascinating that researchers are finally getting back to the work along these lines that was done back in the 1970s -- biofeedback researchers found that people can learn to consciously control their blood pressure, blood glucose, and other biological factors if they get real-time feedback. Of course that research was dropped like a hot rock -- the worst nightmare of the medical industry, after all, is people who take control of their own health -- but it's still out there, and still worth reading.

comments.title: Solstice turning
comments.content:
There's a tradition that whatever you're doing when the New Year is rung in will color what you'll be doing for the whole year to come. I only ever took that seriously on the last year I suffered through a Christmas with my family. That year, I realized that sitting around with unhappy relatives, watching manic revelers on a television screen, and toasting some ball dropping somewhere, with store-bought eggnog from a carton, was not the color I wanted tinting my next year. So, at 10PM on New Year’s Eve I repacked my suitcase and drove to the Blue Ridge. Car camping that night, waking up in the misty wonder of a frozen forest, and hiking through great thickets of rhododendron was the perfect complimentary color to my family's ritual of misery.

My question, though, isn't about New Year's, but about the winter solstice as the astronomical turning of the year. Have you ever heard of the idea that what you're doing when the year turns will color the year ahead getting applied to the solstice? Also, is there some significant energetic turning that occurs at the precise moment of the solstice? I had previously thought of it as a barely incremental shift that then increased momentum as it approached the equinox.

I ask because I received a call on December 21st at 10:42AM from an administrator at an organization I still belonged to, informing me that my membership was terminated, I was barred from the premises, and I was cancelled in every way they thought was important. That organization is now completely dependent on government subsidies, so it has recently become woker-than-thou. The administrator thought it very important to get me to confess to a laundry list of sins he had decided I was guilty of — that's what kept him on the phone, despite his obvious discomfort in talking to one as abhorrent as myself. The PMC don't seem to get their desired energy rush from cancelling unless their target grovels along with the script. I was not about to confess to anyone else's fantasy crimes, so I just kept asking him how the situation had escalated in so methodically calculated a fashion, and why none of the staff had attempted any de-escalation techniques whatsoever.

For the first 15-20 minutes the administrator was a caricature of resistance. Then, sometime awfully close to the exact solstice, the conversation turned and he was complimenting me on my handling of my termination, which he now deeply regretted, and asking me for advice on fixing shortcomings in the organization. The timing was quite odd.

The whole call lasted 54 minutes, and by the end he was complimenting me using terminology and turns of phrase which I tend to use, but had not used in this my first conversation with him. These were odd phrases that I rarely hear others use, especially not blow-hard administrators who have risen above their level of competency. It really seemed like his attempt to ensorcel me had backfired completely and led to him being ensorcelled instead.

That certainly wasn't how I was planning to mark the exact arrival of the solstice, but it does leave me wondering whether the mid-winter turning might be far more significant than I ever realized. Does that kind of abrupt shift from power-drunk ambition by the darkness to sudden, unexpected enlightening line up with the energetic signature of the solstice? And is there any mythic justification for hoping this particular new year’s energy might travel with me, coloring the entire year to come?

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Solstice turning
replies.content: The winter solstice is an extremely important turning point. Its entire symbolism focuses on the birth of light in darkness, so that conversation was appropriate. As for the tradition that what you do at New Years will shape the year to come, I've seen that applied many times to whatever the official new year is, but I don't think I've yet heard of them applied to the solstice. Still, see what happens!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's not a stupid question, it's one of the things you have to be taught. When you divide the deck prior to shuffling it, rotate the half of the deck in one hand so that the end that was up is down, and vice versa. Then shuffle the cards. Repeat the rotation every time you divide the deck, and you'll get everything nicely mixed up in terms of upright and reversed position.

It's not just you! I've had something that visual "overlay" (among other weird visual artefacts) all my life. One of the reasons I hang out here, is that someone will occasionally mention something that makes a little sense of it, in a way that isn't "it's all in your head" or "You're a nutter" :)

I've always wondered if I was perceiving things that are objectively real, but just not typically visible, or if it is just a case of atypical neurology-- a meaningless firing-off of signals from neurons that didn't get adequately pruned in infancy. It has been found, in at least one study, that people who get migraines (I do) react differently to visual stimuli than do non-migraineurs, even when they are not suffering from a migraine. I think this is related, but don't have all the puzzle pieces worked out.

When I was younger, I leaned toward the "neurology" explanation. These days, I lean the other way.

One thing I found was that all of these visual phenomena become more extreme and more distracting if I am following a vegetarian diet, or fasting for more than 24 hours. Eating meat puts a welcome damper on it. Also there are some foods that (for me at least) are like throwing gasoline on that fire-- back in my vegetarian years, I could reliably induce hallucinations by eating tomato paste or taking an aspirin. Some other foods had a similar (but smaller) effect. It may have been a salicylate thing? Now that I'm not a vegetarian, I'm much less sensitive to foods that way. If you find the visual stuff is variable, it might be worth paying attention to what (and when?) you're eating, and seeing if there are things that frequently precede either the headaches or the visual stuff.

I haven't ever found a way to completely avoid headaches, or "cure" the visual weirdness, but eating red meat turns the volume down.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I received a fascinating surprise book for Christmas called "The Book of Fate". Allegedly, it was a divination system used by Napolean, though that is likely just a mythological veneer.

https://publicdomainreview.org/collection/napoleon-s-oraculum-1839

The three curious things about it were:

1. The use of a 5 line binary system, compared to say geomancy's 4 line symbolic system leading to a total of 32 different configurations
2. The use of an xy graph based on the type of question and resulting symbol leading to the answer/fortune
3. The use of masonic imagery throughout the book and especially the graph itself.

I've never heard of any potential relation of Napolean to any sort of occult groups. My first guess was that this work is related to the french occult scene of the early 19th century and was tangentially related to geomancy itself, given the configurations physical likeness to the geomantic figures.

Are you familiar with it at all?

Tamanous

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. "Napoleon's Book of Fate," as it's sometimes called, has been in circulation for at least two hundred years in various formats and versions; my wife, who's interested in such things, has five copies of different eras and variations. It almost certainly has nothing to do with Napoleon. There were a lot of oracle systems back in the day that used the xy-graph approach to giving you answers from a table; the quasi-geomantic figures in the Book of Fate are a unique twist on that approach. Have fun with it!

Many Baroque period instruments groups play at a lower pitch, such as A = 415 Hz.
I happened to read the following today in Jan Fries Dragon Bones: Ritual, Myth and Oracle in Shang Period China:
'Every Chinese emperor began his reign by asking his master musician to specify the basic tone, from which the scales were measured. By the time of the late Zhou the musicians even had special bells cast and tuned, so the basic tone could be shared all over the domain. It made the whole country resonate in the same vibration. Or at least it was meant to... The reason behind this act was the magical nature of music. Music was an essential element in sacrifices and rituals, be it in China or elsewhere, and it still is. Through much of the Shang dynasty, ancestors and deities received regular offerings of panpipe and drum music. The oracle bones even tell us that the spirit of Yi Yin was placated by panpipes and dancing... music, song and dance invited, placated and strengthened the gods, ancestors and spirits. They harmonised relations between heaven, earth and underworld and made the animals (spirits?) dance. Good music even caused the phoenix to descend. For this reason, ancient classics, such as the Lüshi and the Lijing, are full of chapters emphasising the importance of music. From the character of its ritual music, experts could judge the state of a country.'

comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer,

I'm interested in comparing the different goals or aims of initiatory systems. You've indicated before that there can be failed initiations, or initiations that don't take. Comparing the two systems of initiation presented in The Way of the Golden Section and in The Mysteries of Merlin, 1) what are the differences in the results or effects of those two systems, and 2) what could be the signs in each that the process has not been successful?

Regards, and thank you for this forum,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Initiations
replies.content: The Golden Section system is a basic initiation into occult study and practice. You know it doesn't take if you lose interest in occultism and drift away from doing anything related to it. The Merlin work is a somewhat more advanced initiatory cycle meant to awaken the subtle bodies gently through regular practice; you know it doesn't take if after several cycles, the rituals feel dry and boring.

comments.title: Hermes hexagrams, recap
comments.content:
So two or three times I’ve done the Mercury hexagram invocation we’ve discussed, and it feels good. I want to make this a part of my regular practice.

What about the color of visualization? So far I’ve visualized the hexagrams in silver, like the track of a Fourth-of-July silver sparkler. But in The Art of True Healing Israel Regardie says the colors of Mercury are yellow (positive) and orange (negative), positive meaning the color you visualize when you wish to send Mercury energy to someone else (presumably with helpful intentions) and negative when you need assistance from Mercury yourself; or so I understand. Therefore following Regardie I suppose I would be visualizing the hexagrams in orange, as I wish assistance both with legal matters relating to my tenancy, and also with success in a business I am planning soon to launch. Though I must confess I’m rather fond of the silver.

Do you have an opinion on this? Will one color visualization be more efficacious than another? Or is it a matter of preference, given these choices?

replies.title: Re: Hermes hexagrams, recap
replies.content: You can do either one. If you visualize it in silver, imagine it as quicksilver, like liquid mercury, and that'll amplify the effect.

comments.title: Heavy Lifting
comments.content:
Hi JMG. I've been remembering more of some strange incidents that occured in my younger days.
When I was a teenager in Ireland in the 90's,we were in a dimly lit snooker hall with some friends,just hanging about.
Someone suggested we do this trick. The largest guy in our year was sitting in a stool in a corner,we were instructed by this friend to all put our hands over each other above his head.
I think we either counted or chanted something.
There were four of us,we each put two fingers either under the back of his knee or in an armpit,we then were told to lift up.
I distinctly remember my teenage self recoiling at the warmth in his armpit!
Anyway it felt like I was barely pressing upwards,but upwards he went/floated ..about a foot,then we were told to let him down gently or it could really hurt him

Ever heard of this?
Thx
Dylan

replies.title: Re: Heavy Lifting
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. I think that's done in every kids' summer camp in North America. It's really remarkable, isn't it? No, I don't know how it works...

replies.title: Re: Heavy Lifting
replies.content:
I never went to summer camp but I’ve seen the same trick at parties. My guess is that it works because the person’s weight is properly distributed. That’s only a guess, from a kitten who got C’s in Science. Maybe the Great Khan who builds buildings, and thus knows what you can accomplish with the forces of physics, will chime in and enlighten us.

—Princess Cutekitten
